Scenario.overview: Healthcare and Public Health Intermediate The Frankenstein campaign targeted healthcare employees using spear-phishing techniques to gain initial access, then employed PowerShell for command execution, created scheduled tasks for persistence, bypassed UAC for privilege escalation, signed malicious files to evade defenses, and ultimately exfiltrated sensitive data.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Web server services such as emails and databases, Shared DB or directory, Authentication issues
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Email, Web, Phishing, Vulnerability exploit

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2025-5777 [CWE-22]: Spear phishing vulnerability exploited to deliver malicious attachment
stage.description: The attackers crafted personalized emails targeting healthcare employees, exploiting a vulnerability (CVE-2025-5777) to deliver a malicious attachment via email.

stage.name: Execution
stage.surface: Web server services such as emails and databases
stage.kev: CVE-2021-38648 [CWE-94]: PowerShell remoting vulnerability exploited to execute commands
stage.description: Upon successful delivery of the malicious attachment, PowerShell was used to execute commands and download additional payloads, exploiting a vulnerability (CVE-2021-38648) in PowerShell remoting.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: The attackers created scheduled tasks to ensure persistence and re-execute the malware upon system reboot, allowing them to maintain access to shared databases and directories.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: To gain higher privileges, the attackers exploited vulnerabilities to bypass User Account Control (UAC), enabling them to perform unauthorized actions within the authentication process.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.surface: Web server services such as emails and databases
stage.description: To evade detection by security software, the attackers signed malicious files with valid certificates, making them appear legitimate.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC-24: Filter Failure through Buffer Overflow
stage.description: The final stage involved exfiltrating sensitive patient data and intellectual property from shared databases and directories to external servers controlled by the attacker.

Scenario.summary: The Frankenstein campaign demonstrated expert-level skills by employing a multi-stage attack against the healthcare industry, ultimately resulting in the exfiltration of sensitive data.
